data.title: IQI: Discount Narrowing Drives Results On The Back Of Increased Distribution
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Municipal bonds tend to be fairly uneventful investments, which is one of their key selling features; that, and the federally tax-free interest payments they produce as well. In the closed-end fund structure, it does add a couple of additional factors to consider. That includes being leveraged and the discount/premium mechanic, which both can provide different opportunities.

One of the leveraged muni-focused CEFs is the Invesco Quality Municipal Income Trust (NYSE:IQI). The fund trades at a relatively attractive discount, but it is off the wider discount levels it was at in our prior update. Driving the discount to narrow was the substantial distribution increase the fund put into place; however, we'll also be discussing the largest shortfall in distribution coverage that also caused.

IQI Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.77
  • Discount/Premium: -7.06%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.99%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.91%
  • Leverage: 32.79%
  • Managed Assets: $806.22 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

IQI's investment objective is "to provide current income which is exempt from federal income tax." To achieve that objective, they invest in "a diversified portfolio composed substantially of investment-grade tax-exempt municipal securities."

A look at the credit quality with the fund's latest update, and we can see that the fund is overwhelmingly invested in investment-grade issuances, as the investment policy would suggest. There is only a rather small sleeve of 5.80% allocated to what would be considered junk-rated debt.

Looking further into the fund's holdings and the largest allocation is to local general obligation bonds, primarily allocated to California, Texas and New York. This isn't necessarily that unusual, as these are the largest issuers of muni bonds, primarily due to having relatively larger state economies.

An additional benefit for an investor in IQI is that a portion of the distributions paid may be free from state tax obligations as well, for those who reside in states with income taxes.

When including the fund's leverage expenses, we see a total expense ratio come to 2.95%. Leverage increases volatility and the risk of owning this fund; however, it can also result in the potential for enhanced returns.

Narrowing Discount Drives Results

During our last update, which was around two years ago now, the fund's discount has narrowed quite materially. It took around a year before the fund's discount started to narrow off the double-digit level, but that was one of the more attractive characteristics of the fund at that time.

When looking at it over the longer term, this has put the fund's discount back to around its historical average level. That could make it somewhat fairly attractive, but it really is no bargain that it was previously.

That narrowing discount since our last update has helped the fund's overall total returns when compared to the fund's total NAV returns. In addition, it also performed well against the iShares National Muni Bond ETF (MUB).

Overall, MUB is a non-leveraged exchange-traded fund ("ETF"), which can help illustrate relative performance for the diversified muni bond fund space. During this time, neither produced anything that was necessarily brag-worthy, but outsized total returns aren't going to be something that muni funds tend to produce anyway.

Being that it is a leveraged fund, one may have wished to see better overall total NAV returns for taking the additional risk. However, during this higher interest rate environment, leverage costs and management fees generally exceed the yields that munis can pay. That means that during this environment, the added leverage isn't directly resulting in positive income generation due to the negative spread.

On the other hand, the total share price returns were much more attractive, and that did produce outsized returns on a relative basis.

Distribution Bump Drives Discount Narrowing

As we could see above, the fund's discount narrowed rather rapidly, and that wasn't a coincidence.

It was around that time that IQI lifted its distribution by a whopping ~57%. That was after the fund had continually cut its distribution heading into that period. At $0.0631 per month, it sent it back to the highest level in terms of the monthly payout since 2016/2017.

While the discount narrowing is a positive for investors who picked up shares during the double-digit discount slump, there could be some concern now. The fund didn't suddenly see a spike in income generation from its underlying portfolio; instead, they chose to boost the payout specifically to see the fund's discount narrow. That means that the current distribution rate of ~8% isn't being supported entirely.

Based on the latest annual report for the fiscal year-end 2025, net investment income distribution coverage for the 12 months came to around 64%. Coverage would have been a bit lower, but the increased distribution didn't kick in until June 2024.

As the Fed cut 100 basis points in late 2024 and is expected to cut a couple of more times this year, that could see NII coverage improve.

That said, based on the latest quarterly UNII report, coverage is floating at a touch above 61%. That was for the first quarter of 2025, where the first 100 basis points of cuts are already being factored in.

As of their last report, borrowings were through tender option bond trusts ("TOBs") and carried costs at an average interest rate of 4.37%. They also have variable-rate muni term preferred shares outstanding. Those are based on Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association Municipal Swap Index ("SIFMA") plus 1.10%. That worked out to an average annualized dividend rate of 4.25%. Not too far off from the borrowing costs through their TOB.

While that was the borrowing costs of the funds leverage, the 30 day SEC yield for MUB comes to 3.84%. That's a measurement of what MUB has actually received in terms of interest and the yield that works out to from its underlying portfolio. Therefore, we can see why the fund would have been cutting their distribution while short-term rates were rising, increasing their borrowing costs. That's why we saw the fund's NII go from $0.62 in 2021 and gradually decrease to $0.43 in fiscal 2024.

The good news is that SIFMA today is 1.74%, so we would have actually seen that rate drop quite meaningfully to 2.84% for at least a portion of their borrowings. That would suggest that there is some positive spread between the borrowing costs and what the fund is likely able to yield from its muni portfolio. With further cuts from the Fed on their benchmark rate, that would likely result in even lower borrowing rates.

Unfortunately, the other bad news is that the fund has operating expenses, which include the management fee. Those worked out to 0.91%, which would be put on top of the borrowing costs. So we still aren't really seeing a huge benefit from borrowings, but we are starting to get there.

Right now, the biggest benefit of the leverage is that the managers are earning higher fees on the greater AUM. For investors, it is also the potential for upside participation should long-term rates decline, which would see the underlying munis likely appreciate. As is generally the case with muni-focused funds, they carry long maturities with relatively lower yields, which results in them being quite interest rate sensitive as long-duration assets.

The average maturity is 19.22 years for their muni holdings with an option-adjusted duration of 9.93 years. That means that for every 1% change in rates, we should see the fund move nearly 10%—that would be either up or down.

We saw a number of Nuveen funds do this around the same time as well. These moves were more specifically to narrow the underlying discounts that these funds were trading at. Though it took some pressure from activists for this to occur as well.

For IQI, it was Karpus Management that had been building a position. Since then, they have reduced their stake a bit. Unsurprisingly, it was around last year when the fund's discount narrowed meaningfully.

Bank of America (BAC) also had a position that they closed out, but they are only passive investors anyway.

Given the lack of distribution coverage, it isn't going to be too surprising that we see return of capital in the distribution's classification. Not all ROC is negative, but in this case, it is coming from the lack of distribution coverage.

ROC can have its own tax-friendly characteristic as it defers tax obligations, but that is through reducing an investor's cost basis. At some point, that could result in capital gains having to be paid in the future if sold at a gain. So that changes the dynamic of the fund from being primarily tax-free distribution driven to a combination of tax-free and tax-deferred.

Conclusion

We saw a massive increase in the fund's distribution, and that helped to drive the fund's discount to narrow. That in turn also helped to see the fund's total share price results drive higher as well. However, instead of providing a mostly tax-free distribution to its investors, it now comes with a combination of tax-free and tax-deferred distributions. That would be the result of no longer supporting the distribution with the actual income generation from the underlying munis within the portfolio any longer as they overpay.

That said, the good news is that the fund's discount is still at a decent level compared to its historical average. Further, if the Fed cuts its target rate further, the leverage could start contributing positively and result in increased NII. An additional factor is that if long-term risk-free rates also decline, we could see the potential upside from the likelihood of appreciation from those underlying munis in the portfolio.

As we know, this isn't the only muni fund that has now taken this approach do to a wave of funds seeing activist pressure last year. While I think a fixed-income fund covering its payout from income is more attractive, I think that if investors know what they are getting into, it can still be okay to hold. Additionally, the discount overall is still relatively decent for a 'Hold' rating here.

data.title: Merlin Properties: Crudely Around 20% NAV Upside Before Tax Considerations
data.description:
MERLIN Properties SOCIMI, S.A. (OTCPK:MRPRF) is continuing to grow rents and FFO, and the matter of the SOCIMI rule changes seems to be put to bed for the time being with no new mention of it and it not making it into legislation for tax reform. The asset value of their emerging data center business is leading the rent growth contribution figures, which makes sense for an emerging segment that is taking the bulk of their CAPEX commitments. It also means that rent growth is very likely to follow, even just on the existing investments. We think that datacenters continue to be a reasonably interesting area, but we are not entirely convinced that it won't become an industry that will end up in oversupply, considering the real innovation now in AI is going to be on the interfacing with hardware in our opinion, pointing at DeepSeek. On a discount from NAV (net asset value) basis, there is around 20% upside for Merlin. If their ongoing investments are profitable, they are of course capable of growing their GAV (gross asset value) nicely. Quite a lot of their CAPEX is on development, which could return substantially well. Normally, a 20% upside on a NAV discount basis wouldn't be that interesting, but in all likelihood their significant planned CAPEX should generate meaningful value in datacenters. Compared to a typical holding box, Merlin should be able to reinvest (or, given their REIT-equivalent structure, raise and invest) cash as better than average rates.

Latest Earnings

There weren't that many asset rotations in the Q1. Just around 37 million EUR, but the premiums were decent in the low double digits, and ahead of costs of capital in the current environment. Currently, quite a lot of development and acquisitions are on data centers. They are built but not yet yielding, hence the discrepancy between rents and GAV contribution as of the FY. Now, with construction and commercialization risks behind that first wave of assets, there could be some revaluations as discount rates on those assets fall. But current NAV stands at around 8 billion EUR on the 6.7 billion EUR market cap, so an upside of 20%.

Pending CAPEX on the datacenter business is around 2 billion EUR, and a lot of it will be focused on greenfield development, which should yield even better on investment. Current rent growth is 2.7% overall for the business, but just from currently booked GAV of datacenter assets, we should expect growth in rents from datacenters proportional to their GAV contribution in the rent figures as the capacity becomes delivered and rents begin to be earned. The current installed capacity is still at 1/3rd of the expected for Phase I investments, expected to be completed and mostly delivered by 2027. With Phase II, datacenters are expected to be around 20% of the GAV. This could be achieved before 2030. Part of Phase I's around 20 MW of installed capacity should be delivered soon and start to earn rents.

Bottom Line

In terms of impacts from the Trump administration, the only thing really mentioned is that there was a dial-back of plans to invest in the datacenter footprint in Portugal due to some possible consequences from changes expected from the Trump administration of the AI Diffusion Act. However, it didn't seem to warrant a separate release, with plans instead to increase investments in Spain instead to offset the declines in Portugal. Ultimately, it's more of a shift in timelines and less a determined reduction in how much the company plans to invest overall, which is good to hear because there are some scale economies when it comes to how a datacenter is supposed to use the provided infrastructure.

Merlin remains alright. We don't think the 20% upside on the NAV discount is enough to be interested because it also doesn't take into account tax effects. But in general, we think that the development return on investments in these developments in the datacenter business in particular should yield overall GAV gain and, of course, we should see topline growth as these projects become delivered. A lot of the capacity that is in the process of being built is already pre-let, so we can be confident in at least several points of topline growth driven just by datacenter.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SoFi: Giant Banks Are Betting Big On Innovation
data.description:
Executive Summary

SoFi Technologies (NASDAQ:SOFI) tanked by 13% after I released my previous analysis, which still did not make the stock attractively valued. Even applying aggressive business expansion assumptions does not justify SOFI's current market cap, per my calculations. In addition, there are several fundamental risks that can potentially undermine SOFI's ability to maintain business growth over the long haul. The competition is fierce, and SOFI does not seem to have any unmatched strategic advantages. As a result, this stock remains a SELL, in my opinion.

SOFI Analysis

I still believe that SOFI's future business expansion prospects are very cloudy because the financial services industry is an extremely competitive environment. There are thousands of commercial banks in the U.S., and SOFI is not even close to the top-10 publicly traded banks and consumer finance companies in terms of market cap.

The industry's largest player, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) is aggressively driving innovation. This giant drives a massive multi-faceted innovation strategy, which will likely make its offerings more compelling for younger generations. This is a real threat for SOFI, especially considering JPM's vast client base.

JPM has various promising fintech-related endeavors, which are aimed at accelerating the shift to become a modern bank. The banking behemoth recently started a strategic partnership with the AI/ML platform Cleareye to streamline trade finance operations. Another new partnership with Plaid to unlock open banking is another step making JPM closer to fintech.

The second-largest banking conglomerate in the U.S., Bank of America (BAC), also has quite ambitious plans to modernize its operations. BAC plans to spend $4 billion on AI and new tech initiatives in 2025. This amount is comparable to the entire tangible book value of SOFI ($4.6 billion), which underscores the massive gap in scales of BAC and SOFI. New endeavors are not only expected to leverage cutting-edge technologies to streamline internal processes, but also to improve interaction with clients. Overall, BAC's AI and ML patents portfolio has almost doubled since 2022.

These U.S. banking giants set the tone for all other banks across the world because a recent report from KPMG suggests that the global AI spending by banks is poised to increase in the near future. While most banks plan to optimize costs with the help of AI capabilities, 42% of them expect to enhance customer experience with new technologies. This is a notable percentage of traditional banks that have ambitious to make their interaction with clients more AI-centric, which also creates threats for smaller players like SOFI.

In addition, the macro environment looks quite unfavorable for SOFI. Despite the recently announced trade deal between China and the U.S., there are still risks of an economic slowdown. Jamie Dimon, JPM's CEO, believes that recession risks are still quite high for the U.S. Furthermore, a fresh Reuters poll of economists showed that the outlook for the U.S. economy remains weak. Last but not least, the IMF sharply trimmed their forecast for the U.S. 2025 and 2026 GDP growth. Therefore, the road is likely to be bumpy over the next few years from a macroeconomic perspective.

Thus, it is difficult to be optimistic about SOFI's growth prospects considering the likely macroeconomic weakness in the U.S. coupled with a fierce competition in the industry where large traditional banks have more than enough resources to aggressively drive their innovative endeavors.

Valuation Is Over The Wazoo

SOFI's valuation ratios are over the wazoo, particularly P/E and P/S multiples. A forward P/E ratio of almost 48 suggests that SOFI has to demonstrate extremely aggressive bottom-line growth over the next several years, which is highly uncertain due to fierce competition. Since SOFI is a financial services company, the Price/Book ratio is also crucial. From this perspective, SOFI is valued at a massive premium compared to the sector median.

The DCF approach also revealed significant overvaluation. In the context of a financial service company, the free cash flow and net income are not very far from each other because capital spending and amortization are inherently low for this type of business.

Therefore, the DCF starts with the company's current (TTM) net profit of $481.7 million. A 21.7% bottom line CAGR is applied, aligning with the Wall Street EPS CAGR forecasted by consensus up to 2034. As we mentioned, the industry's fierce competition makes SOFI's growth sustainability highly uncertain, which makes me use a conservative 3% growth rate into perpetuity.

SOFI's total NPV is around $10 billion, substantially below the current $14.7 billion market cap. This outcome confirms takeaways from the previously conducted valuation ratios analysis, meaning that SOFI is indeed massively overvalued. Applying a very aggressive 5% CAGR into perpetuity improves the picture, but SOFI still remains 21% overvalued.

Potential Drawbacks

The extremely high level of uncertainty might work positively for SOFI as well. There is always a possibility that SOFI's competitors might unexpectedly start struggling, which will be favorable for the company in terms of gaining market share. In addition, the company might roll out some disruptive features and offerings, which will significantly boost its value proposition. The probability of such a scenario is quite low, but it is definitely above zero.

SOFI's price demonstrated weakness in 2025 so far, with a 17% decline. At the same time, optimism around the stock remains quite strong. I see this optimism from recent SA articles with only bullish ratings over the last few weeks. Due to its notable fan base, the stock's recent underperformance might be a large sign to load up for bulls. Therefore, a temporary share price rebound might happen soon.

The Verdict

A massive 13.4% short interest in SOFI looks absolutely fair because the stock is massively overvalued with the fair NPV around $10 billion. The company's ability to maintain its business expansion pace is highly uncertain because competition is fierce and will highly likely intensify further. Due to all these fundamental risks, I think that SOFI remains a SELL.

data.title: Grifols: If This Works Out, It Has Massive Upside
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

I have been covering Grifols (NASDAQ:GRFS) for some time now, through the potential of a sale of the entire company, and back again when that sale did not materialize. I view this as a very good thing. In my history of investing, I have no less than 4 times been subject to a superb investment that I wanted to keep being taken private, bought out, or somehow removed in a way that made it impossible for me to invest. The examples have been sectors like IT; the Norwegian company Data Respons, to something as simple as a food staple/grocer, when Swedish market leader ICA AB was taken private and off the market by the collective/cooperative owners.

What I look for in a company is qualities that transcend cycles. Needs met that do not go away, and where the company also has a clear edge. And with Grifols, dear readers, I believe we have exactly that.

That is why I invest. That is why I want more shares in Grifols at a cheap price. And that is why I believe the company has a massive sort of upside. I don't use the term "massive" easily or lightly either. When it comes to this, I believe the potential for upside here is higher than 200% over the long term - and in the article, I will show you exactly what I believe this to come from.

I will also show you an example of when I bought a company at dirt-cheap valuation, only to where it now is trading at almost 6x of this - it's not the first time, nor will it be the last.

But let's first make sure that what we invest in is actually relatively "safe."

Grifols - Update After Q1 2025 Shows Significant Promise

From the small peak in February and my last article, which by the way you can find here, the company is underperforming slightly. Does this bother me? Not in the least.

Why doesn't it bother me?

Because I invest for the long term - the long term in this context meaning 3–5 years, or more. Long-term investments mean that I have a high conviction of the survivability of the company over the longer term.

Grifols has been around for a long time, and as you can see in my last article, is engaged in crucial sectors - namely, plasma and plasma derivatives to treat various conditions and also to supply the world's healthcare systems with blood plasma. The company is one of the largest companies in what it does, and it concentrates its activities mostly in countries where it is not against the law to offer compensation for blood/plasma. This is the case, for instance, in my home nation of Sweden (illegal to pay for plasma).

This means, in such countries as mine, we rely on paying for/importing plasma, but there is no current political movement in making it legal, or in changing the status quo in this. There are many countries like this, and something to keep in mind.

Aside from the core segment of plasma, the company also does R&D and marketing of Diagnostic products. Throughout its history, its growth has been mostly organic, but with inorganic additions, such as Talecris. Plasma collection today, aside from Grifols, is being done by Takeda (TAK) and CSL/Octapharma. It's a small sector, and unlike its smaller peers and fragmented other market competition, Grifols has a comprehensive, global infrastructure for what it does.

The Q1 2025 results confirm to me that the upside for the company is playing along exactly as expected. We have over 7% top-line growth, over 14.2% FCF-like/EBITDA growth YoY, and margin increases of almost 1%. FCF is not yet positive, but improved by over €200M since my last article.

The company's main issue is debt/leverage, not a fun thing to get out of for any company that has overleveraged. It usually takes years - no different for Grifols in this case. The company had leverage of well over 6x only back a year ago - so some of the pressure on the share price is certainly understandable (though I still argue it was being pressured more than was strictly justified). But now, as of Q1 2025, the company's leverage is down below 5x, at 4.5x.

While it is in no way "fixed," I hasten to remind you that the company does not currently pay a dividend and is not a company with negative EPS. In fact, adjusted earnings for 2024A grew by 96% YoY, and for 2025E, the current forecast is another 13-14% adjusted EPS growth. My point is that the company can use this considerable cash/EPS entirely focusing on debt, and at the same rate, chances are we'd be below 3.5-4x by the end of this year.

As we can see above, the company's improvements were broad-based, meaning that all segments, including Biopharma, contributed to growth. In terms of granularity, Albumin continues to be a challenge (see my last article), but growth in Immunoglobulin was massive, over 15% LFL growth YoY. The company is growing ahead of markets, and again, the company's extensive infrastructure across the world is not only supporting growth but also mitigating any potential tariff impact. In the US, for instance, the company's donor centers represent over 70% of the plasma collection capacity in the entire nation. The company's two manufacturing plants account for 65% of global fractionation. It's unfair to say that without Grifols, the U.S./NA would be in trouble, but it certainly would not be easy.

What I want to focus on in Q1 2025 is not recent trends or company fundamentals, though - I see those as relatively well-established. Instead, I want to highlight the degree of outperformance over time, in recovery, that the company has seen. The improvements have, in my view, confirmed that this is more than just a one-time thing, it's a clear pattern of recovery that supports the company once again delivering strong shareholder returns, as it has in the past.

And as investors, we're still able to get in on the "ground floor," even if it's not at record-cheap levels. What I especially want to highlight is the improvement in free cash flow, coming from clearly better trends in working capital, offsetting the impact of current CapEx and interest phasing. Another quarter like this with similar trends, and the company will be cash-flow positive.

The company's recovery and trajectory to once more become a dividend-paying and attractive company is still a long one, over about a year or two. However, what I see in earnings trends and company execution cannot be denied. Risks to the company are, as I will argue now, low. The risks to the company usually mentioned are usually operational and related to previous fundamentals. What I mean by this is that they are the high leverage and the lack of a dividend. Obviously, this is something that, I believe, will be solved in the near term, and one can argue is already being solved, with a logic of "If A, B", meaning if high leverage, then no dividend, and the opposite being true as well.

Therefore, the one primary risk I would be willing to ascribe to Grifols at this time is related to new technologies and synthetic solutions for its products. Those products are, however, either still under heavy R&D or expensive, and that's also why the company is working on other "arms" for its business.

Q1 2025, as I see it, is a confirmation that the company is on the right path and that the trajectory is continuing. This continues to make Grifols a valuation case.

Grifols - The valuation tells us that an upside of 200% + is possible

If you recall my last article, I gave the company a price target (PT) of €20/share. At one point, this seemed incredibly high, and even today it's a 100% improvement from the current share price of €9.4/share for the native ticker GRF.

However, please realize that at below €10/share, the company's P/E is less than 14x even at 2024-2025E results, and current forecasts are for those results to almost double by 2027.

If this doubling of EPS were to happen, the company's current price would be closer to a 7- 9x P/E. This might be fair according to some, but not to me, for two reasons.

First, the company has a historical average of trading at over a P/E of 26x on a long-term average of 20 years. Even if we discount that, we cannot get away from the company commanding a premium, at least on a historical basis. Secondly, the company is expected to grow over 23% per year in EPS on average for the next 3 years, and hit such targets over 63% of the time with a 10-20% margin of error. It's therefore of high/above-average conviction that the company will do more or less what it forecasts and what analysts forecast.

Based on this, an upside to a 26x P/E here would imply an annualized rate of return of 60% per year, or 242% - and that's with an extremely modest dividend forecast, as you can see below.

It bears mentioning that the company is B+ rated, and thus has ways to go in terms of leverage, and the dividend restoration is currently forecasted for 2026E.

Even if we discount the company to a 15- 18x P/E, that still would mean a 100%+ RoR in total in 3 years, or between 30-40% per year, at a fair value of around €22/share. This would be a very conservative estimate. I believe the fair value is closer to €30-€35/share, which justifies my PT of €20/share, which means buying below €20/share, and then no more. I maintain my PT of €20/share for the native here.

Furthermore, if you look at Seeking Alpha's own grading, you will note that the company scores high on almost every single metric, with the sole issue being the "revision" score, understandable given the current context of reversal. The company is volatile, but has only slightly underperformed the S&P500 here, with potential for outperformance going forward.

I also promised you a company that has seen similar valuation development to what I expect out of Grifols. For that, I would look at Unum (UNM), which I bought during and close to COVID-19 (look at my articles from the time). The company has now improved to an $82/share price, which is obviously a very far cry from the valuation when I did heavy investing. One of my buys was back in August 2020, and here you see the return since that investment (though to be clear, I sold before then and retain only a watchlist position).

So why show this? Two reasons - first, to confirm to you that valuation-oriented investing works when done correctly, and to show you the value of patience above all. We live in a very immediate satisfaction society, and I am going contrary to this (and have for some time). For me, this works - and it could for you as well.

Peers to Grifols are as mentioned. Takeda is the only one relevant. While it has a yield and BBB+ rating, and could be an alternative, I see a few issues that I don't see with Grifols. First, it's premiumized at over 30x P/E. Secondly, it misses negatively over 70% of the time with a 20% margin of error, which leads me to expect more instability here. That being said, Takeda does deserve you looking at it - I just believe Grifols is better for the reasons mentioned.

My thesis for the company is unchanged - it's a buy with the same PT, and strengthened after Q1 2025 due to excellent execution, and I expect even better results going forward. The ADR for Grifols remains GRFS, but remember that the GRFS reflects the preference share, which means that the PT for GRFS still comes to $18.75/share.

Thesis

  • Grifols remains the world leader in Plasma and plasma-related biopharma. This gives the company a clear advantage. However, overleverage has driven the credit rating to a B and canceled the company's relatively acceptable dividend. It means we need to wait for this to recover somewhat, and even if Q1 2025 promises some of this, it's still not entirely there. This is now a better case, with Brookfield having dropped the company as a private buyout target.
  • I can call this non-yielding business a speculative "Buy", despite an upside in the double digits and an "easily" forecastable upside of over 20% per year until 2026E-2027E if the company's expectations materialize. As of the latest results, and 2025E, I believe we're closer than ever to materializing such an upside, with Q1 2025 confirming it.
  • I'm keeping my target for the company as of quarterly results at a €20/share native PT, with a slightly different one for the ADR given the reflection of the preference instead of native share, which means that I'm positive, and own the stock, but I'm staying conservatively exposed to make up for the risk I see here. I might buy more at this time.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators, and 2 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for me to clear my hurdle and invest here. Based on this, and a valuation target of €20/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "BUY" rating as of May 2025.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Stanley Retools Production To Move Out Of China
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Stanley Black and Decker (NYSE:SWK) is the largest hand-tool providers in the world, with brands such as DeWalt, Craftsman, Stanley, and Black and Decker.

On a year-to-date basis SWK has been hammered losing 10.84%, largely due to 50% of its supply chain being exposed to tariffs including 14.5% from China. Management has announced its mitigation plan, with a 24 month shift out of China and ensuring compliance with USMCA in Mexico. Overall, management expects a net effect of $0.75 per share headwind to earnings. Despite these headwinds, the long-term plan for transformation is still in place, with SWK executing $500 million in SG&A cuts and several low-margin product line disposals.

SWK still has strong fundamentals, expecting over $500 million in free cash flow generated during 2025. Thus, we believe the 4.64% dividend is safe at the present moment and allows dividend investors an attractive income while awaiting the continued transformation of the largest tool company in the world.

EFV = E27 EPS X P/E = $6.85 X 13.3x = $91.00

Market Conditions

As we discussed in our Whirlpool article, due to a mixture of tariff fears, high finance rates, and a growing inventory of unsold homes homebuilder confidence has been at its lowest level since August 2012. However, despite some DIY softness management indicated that there was consistent professional demand and inventory levels are consistent with historical levels. While this may hit the income statement as a decrease in volumes, management has stated that favorable currency and SG&A savings should keep margins stable excluding the impact of tariffs.

SWK’s management maintains that it has a “industry-leading” footprint, with 43.5% of cost of sales being components originated from the United States, and 6.2% “USMCA Complaint” from Mexico. However, this does leave 14.5% from China, 12.4% from non-compliant facilities Mexico, and 23.4% from the rest of the world – or a total of 50.3% of cost of sales exposed to tariffs.

Management’s mitigation strategy is going to be implemented over the next 12-24 months. Over the short-term, there were high single digit increases to pricing effective in April, with management evaluating if a second round is needed during the quarter ending September 2025. On the supply side, the first priority is increasing the amount of Mexican supply that is USMCA compliant. Management indicated that becoming USMCA complaint for the rest of the Mexican supply chain is not “operationally complex”, and we believe that SWK should be able to accomplish this by the end of the quarter ending April 2026.

Over the medium-term, there are several products that are dual source in nature and can have supply lines moved from China to Mexico over the next 12 months. Over the long-term, management is targeting a full elimination of China from the supply chain by the 24-month mark (April 2027).

The rest of the world will remain untouched for now, with SWK saying the cost increases can largely be absorbed by price increases passed to consumers.

Tools & Outdoor

Tools and Outdoor represents 86.6% of revenues and includes all of SWK’s brands and hand tools. Most sales are linked to residential or commercial construction activity, with 63% of revenues coming from the US.

For the quarter ending March 2025 revenues were flat overall, with operating margin expanding by 110bps to 9.6%. The increase in margin came from continued savings in the supply chain and new product launches but were weighed down by early tariff impacts and cost of freight increases.

Across the total segment, 5 brands make up 80% of revenues, including DeWalt, Craftsman, Stanley, Black and Decker, and Cub Cadet. The top two tool and outdoor companies, SWK and an unnamed competitor, represent less than 25% of the total market, giving ample opportunity to take market share at scale. The long-term target is to have mid-single digit revenue growth primarily through a more aggressive target marketing toward professionals.

As previously discussed, much of the segment is heavily linked to construction activity. Uncertainty surrounding tariffs, high interest rates, and low demand has created an oversupply of housing at current prices. While we believe this is cyclical, the up part of the cycle is difficult to determine given remodeling and DIY activity still appears robust with the segment reported 2% organic growth in the United States. Overall, we believe that given the strong brand recognition and historical resilience the fundamentals of the business remain strong.

Fastening

The Engineered Fastening (formerly called industrial) segment represents 13.4% of revenues and produces fasteners, rivets, and specialized tools. A plurality of revenue is generated in the automotive industry, with 52% coming from the US.

During the quarter ending March 2025, total revenues fell 21%, though largely this was due to the divestiture of the infrastructure business. Organic revenue fell 1% year over year, due to volume loss from softness in the automotive sector. Excluding the impact of the divestiture, segment operating margins compressed by 200bps to 10.1% largely due to the loss of high-margin volumes from the automotive sector.

Over the short term we do expect some softness in the fastening segment, given the likely slowdown in automotive manufacturing and the 25% steel and aluminum tariffs causing broad pricing increase across the entire supply chain. Current tariffs on autos are at 25% for non-USMCA complaint, however we believe that more tariffs are likely in the pipeline for autos, especially for trucks from Canada and cars from Mexico. It is presently unclear the implementation timeline of additional tariffs, though we believe the most likely scenario is after the USMCA renewal talks in July 2026.

With that said, the long-term fundamentals of the segment are strong. The aerospace area has experienced mid-teens organic growth, with management emphasizing that despite some softness in automotive OEMs related to softening capex, the segment maintains a multi-year backlog.

Risk

While SWK has already implemented price increases, it will likely evaluate for further price increases during the quarter ending September 2025. We are unsure how much of the increased costs SWK will be able to pass on given DIY consumers will cut back on purchases due to broad inflation increases, and commercial customers will cut back as their entire supply chain comes under pressure.

Despite preliminary agreements reached with China and much of Mexican manufacturing trade falling under current USMCA tariff exemptions, we expect tariffs to continue to be a factor over the medium term. Management maintains it will be able to roll-off tariff from operations by 2027 with repositioning efforts.

Financials

Including the impact of tariffs, organic sales growth is expected to be in the low to mid-single digits. This will be driven by a mid-single digit pricing increase, which will likely lead to low single digit volume declines. On a per segment basis, SWK expects less volume impacts in fasteners with the segment still growing in the low to mid-single digit area. Tools and outdoor is expected to lose volume due to mid-single digit price expansion, with the net effect being low single digit expansion.

As of the end of April 2025, SWK assumes headwinds of $0.75 per share to earnings due to the tariffs, representing an increase of costs of approximately $1.7 billion. Management emphasized that it does not expect this full cost to hit the company’s bottom line during 2025 due to SG&A cuts and price increases. Overall, we expect around $1-1.1 billion in tariff impacts to hit the bottom line in 2025, which is in line with management’s estimates.

The worst of the impacts will be in the quarter ending June 2024 due to LIFO (last in first out) accounting. Historically, most cash flows are weighted in the back third of the year, so there is still some time for tariff policy to shake out more firmly. Additionally, the back third weighting allows SWK to interface with customers and retailers to determine specifically how elastic their demand is as mentioned in the risk section.

Overall management believes they can have most of the tariff impact mitigated by 2027. Long-term targets are still unchanged at 35% gross margin and 16-19% EBITDA margin. Primarily, this will be accomplished through SG&A cuts, removing low-margin and low-volume tools, and scale benefits in manufacturing. Over the short term we expect there to be some reversal in progress, though accelerating the pace of ceasing low-margin low-volume products and the previously discussed USCMA compliance actions margins may not compress as far as feared.

Additionally, cost savings programs are underway to shave almost $500 million in SG&A expenses during 2025. To 2027, management is targeting 21.5% of revenue going toward SG&A expenses, currently at 22.5%.

During the year ending December 2024, SWK generated $753 million in free cash. For the full year 2025, management is guiding toward ‘at least’ $500 million in free cash flow, though specifics of how tariffs and consumer sensitivity will impact free cash flow remains to be seen.

As of the quarter ending March 2025, SWK has $344.8 million in cash on hand. Currently, the dividend yield is $3.28 annually or 4.64%. This represents a payout ratio of approximately 72%, which we believe is safe even factoring in tariff impacts.

The current debt to EBITDA for the trailing twelve months is 4.4x, with the long-term target being 2.0 to 2.5x. Current debt outstanding has a weighted average interest 3.9% with an average term remaining of 13 years. Since 2021 management has been authorized to repurchase $3 billion in shares, though it has not executed on this and we do not believe it will until it reaches its leverage target.

Conclusion

While there are short-term uncertainties associated with tariff policy, management has laid out a transparent plan to address its impacts to the bottom line. By 2027, we believe that management will be able to execute its plans of removing manufacturing from China and becoming fully USMCA compliant for operations in Mexico. Given the scale in which it operates as the largest tool brand in the world, it will be able to weather any economic fallout better than peers in our opinion.

Based on the expected $500+ million in expected free cash for 2025 despite tariffs, we believe that the dividends 4.64% yield is safe. SWK has seen a price decline of 20.41% over the trailing twelve months, and thus the mixture of tariff deals appearing more likely and structural cost savings program mark an attractive entry point for dividend investors.

data.title: Apple's Biggest Risk Isn't Competition--It's The Consumer
data.description:
Thesis

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:AAPL) is a monument to scale, taste, and optimization, a brand that took consumer electronics and turned them into personal identity statements. Its ascension has been driven by its ability to sell the idea of elegant devices, priced accordingly. Yet as we move deeper into a macroeconomic regime shift defined by structural uncertainty and behavioral retreat, that model now finds itself exposed. Not broken, necessarily, but misaligned. What once felt frictionless now feels contingent.

At the heart of this tension is a simple truth: Apple’s core offerings: iPhones, Macs, and iPads are discretionary, not essential. And they’re financed accordingly. Whether through Apple Card Monthly Installments or third-party retail plans, much of Apple’s revenue machine is powered not by the willingness to pay, and by the belief that the financial scaffolding will hold. That belief, however, is unraveling in slow motion.

A Textbook Quarter

Apple Inc. reported what, on paper, appears to be another quarter of frictionless growth. Revenue came in at $95.36 billion, which was up 5.08% year-over-year and beating expectations by $652 million. Earnings per share came in at $1.65, up 8% year-over-year, a record for the March quarter. Net income stood at $24.8 billion. Operating cash flow came in at a strong $24 billion. It was, by any standard quarterly scorecard, a textbook example of execution.

And yet, there is a creeping dissonance that belies the headline strength. It’s not just that the economic backdrop is softening; it’s that the very architecture of Apple’s growth model may be colliding, almost systematically, with macroeconomic, financial, and policy forces moving in the opposite direction.

And this, right here, is where the gears start to grind.

Apple’s core product ecosystem–iPhones, Macs, iPads–is constructed on aesthetic desire and discretionary surplus. These are high-ticket items that people buy not because they have to, but because they can. Or more precisely because they believe they can, often with the help of installment financing. Apple’s Apple Card Monthly Installments plan, for instance, pitches customers zero percent APR to stretch a $1,200 iPhone into manageable bites. But zero percent only works in a zero-ish rate world. And that world is over.

Rising Rate Reality

As of May 21, 2025, the 30-year Treasury yield moved past 5.089%, its highest since the liquidity spasms of October 2023 and the 10-year yield climbed to 4.595%, while the 2-year edged back above 4%, reaching 4.01%. These are, generally speaking, red-alert signals for the real economy.

That's because treasury yields set the floor for virtually all consumer interest rates like mortgages, auto loans, credit cards, and yes, the kind of point-of-sale financing that Apple’s retail model increasingly leans on. So as yields rise, so do APRs. Lenders reprice risk, underwriting standards tighten, and suddenly that monthly iPhone payment gets a little harder to swallow.

And this tightening is not happening in a vacuum. The economic backdrop is sliding into a kind of slow-motion fiscal panic. A weak auction of 20-year Treasury bonds on the same day yields spiked triggered a visible shift in investor sentiment. The problem was what that weak demand implied: market participants were growing uneasy about how much debt the U.S. government intended to issue and what it would take to convince buyers to absorb it.

The backdrop behind all this is a set of proposed multitrillion-dollar tax cuts currently being floated by House Republicans and President Trump, which would add significantly to the $36 trillion national debt. So much for DOGE. But smart investors know the math: more Treasury issuance means more supply. More supply requires higher yields. Higher yields ripple into higher costs of capital across the board.

While interest rates climb and credit tightens, it’s the collapse of confidence, the invisible fulcrum of consumption I've seen play out now and again that may ultimately pose the more immediate threat to Apple’s growth thesis. As of May 2025, the University of Michigan’s Consumer Sentiment Index had cratered to 50.8, marking its second-lowest level ever recorded and reflecting a nearly 30% collapse since January.

And what’s bubbling up beneath is a flip of human nature, that moment when the great thundering herd of consumers, en masse, shift from spending to saving.

Behavioral Shift

Carl Weinberg, chief economist at High Frequency Economics, describes this not as a panic, but as a recalibration. Households, he notes, are deliberately shifting toward caution, deferring large purchases, pulling back on discretionary outlays, and saving more. And in practice, that means fewer people see a $1,200 iPhone or a $2,000 MacBook as a smart purchase. What was once framed as a default lifestyle upgrade is increasingly being reclassified, psychologically, as a luxury to be postponed, if not skipped entirely.

Adding fuel to the fire are consumers’ own expectations. Year-ahead inflation expectations have surged to 7.3%, the highest reading since 1981. Long-term expectations are also on the rise, climbing to 4.6%. These are emotional inputs. In other words, when consumers expect prices to rise faster than wages or returns, their default response is to wait. To hoard cash. To avoid the very kind of big-ticket spending that companies like Apple have optimized their product pipeline and financing infrastructure around.

Meanwhile, indicators of near-term and forward-looking sentiment are in freefall. The current economic conditions index has slumped to 57.6, and the consumer expectations index, effectively a pulse-check on household confidence in the future, has fallen to 46.5. These are numbers that imply the formation of a consumer psychology that is defensive, skeptical, and risk-averse. In this mindset, even an interest-free installment plan on a new device begins to look like a bad decision.

Retail Retrenchment

Take Fitch Ratings, for instance. The agency downgraded its outlook for the U.S. retail and consumer products sector to “deteriorating” in the last month due to waning consumer sentiment and weaker upgrade cycles in discretionary categories. The agency highlighted less compelling upgrade cycles, which has more to do with desire softening than with innovation stalling.

The ripple effects of this are easy to trace. Chanel, a bastion of luxury pricing power and Asian growth, saw operating profits plunge nearly a third last year to $4.5 billion. Sales fell 4.3%, marking its first simultaneous decline in both revenue and earnings since 2020. A 7.1% drop in Asia alone underscored the fragility of what had once been considered an untouchable growth engine. At the other end of the spectrum, Walmart (WMT) reported a 4.5% increase in U.S. same-store sales, a result that might look great until you read the subtext that the company warned of “substantial uncertainty” going forward, citing inflation and volatile tariff conditions that could distort future demand. Meanwhile, Target (TGT), which sits squarely in the discretionary middle, reported a 3.8% decline in same-store sales, with notable softness in electronics and other high-ticket items. Its guidance was slashed, its tone cautious, and its explanation familiar: inflation worries and declining consumer appetite.

Apple’s product set is not immune to this shift in psychology, and cracks are emerging even within its latest earnings. The company’s product gross margin fell to 35.9%, a sequential decline of 340 basis points and a year-over-year drop of 70 basis points. Management attributed the decline to “mix,” foreign exchange pressures, and seasonal loss of leverage. But this kind of language increasingly reads as a euphemism for deteriorating pricing power. Yes, the Services gross margin expanded to 75.7%, offsetting some of the damage. But this only highlights the growing reliance on recurring revenue streams to balance out an increasingly margin-pressured hardware business.

And then there are Wearables, Home, and Accessories, the same segment that once symbolized Apple’s seamless expansion beyond the iPhone. It posted a 5% year-over-year revenue decline to $7.5 billion. Even as the Apple Watch installed base hit an all-time high, and hearing features in AirPods Pro gained traction, the numbers show slowing volume growth, mounting competition, and what looks like consumer fatigue in what was once a robust growth engine.

China Exposure

China adds another layer of fragility. Revenue in Greater China fell 2% year-over-year. Apple tried to massage the number, noting that FX-neutral results were closer to flat. But even that modest defense came with caveats. The company acknowledged that performance was buoyed by government subsidies, especially for lower-priced iPhones under RMB6,000. The premium-tier devices, those with higher margins and ASPs, remain vulnerable not only to consumer pullback but to Beijing’s shifting subsidy posture and rising techno-nationalist sentiment. With China still serving as both a key market and a primary source of manufacturing, Apple remains exposed at both ends of the supply chain.

That supply chain is under stress as well.

Tariff costs are creeping in, and they are no longer abstract. Apple estimates a $900 million increase in the cost of goods sold in the June quarter, attributed directly to tariffs. The company’s CFO cautioned analysts not to extrapolate this into future quarters, noting that “certain unique factors” such as build-ahead inventory helped cushion the immediate blow. But the implication is clear: the risks are real, and the company is already rearranging operations to blunt them. Apple is shifting U.S.-bound production of iPhones to India and Macs and iPads to Vietnam. But products sold outside the U.S., especially AppleCare and accessories, are still mainly sourced from China and now face tariffs of up to 145%.

Valuation Disconnect

Some of our own analysts on Seeking Alpha have already moved to the “sell” side. Right now, Apple’s blended P/E sits at 28.8x, well above its long-term average of 23.52x, and even further from FAST Graphs’ fair value estimate of 15.0x.

That model puts Apple’s fair value at $105.26 a share, which would mean close to a 48% drop from where it trades today. The logic isn’t complicated: the market’s starting to price in slower growth, and the multiple is coming down to meet it.

Apple’s trailing P/E of 29.94x in 2024 isn’t holding. It’s expected to slide year after year: 28.10x in 2025, 25.69x in 2026, 23.25x in 2027, and finally landing at 19.84x by 2028. What’s notable is that this decline in valuation comes alongside rising earnings. In other words, the market doesn’t seem to believe the growth story is worth paying a premium for anymore. And it's not just about multiples.

Net income over the last twelve months is $97.3 billion, still massive, but off its 2022 peak of $99.8 billion.

Operating cash flow tells the same: $109.6 billion now, down from $122.1 billion. Even as levered free cash flow holds at $97.25 billion, the per-share figure has slipped from a peak of $7.09 in 2024 to $6.51 today. Meanwhile, cash taxes paid have ballooned from $26.1 billion to $43.3 billion over the trailing twelve months, an increase that may reflect tighter global tax enforcement, repatriation effects, or less favorable R&D regimes.

And still, Apple has returned capital at a rate that borders on aggressive. In the past year, it sent $122.2 billion back to shareholders, $106.9 billion via share repurchases, and $15.3 billion in dividends. The company also sold $26.3 billion in marketable securities, suggesting that even Apple has begun cannibalizing its cash position to maintain its return cadence. Its capital expenditures stood at just $11.07 billion, and no meaningful M&A was undertaken. The conclusion is unavoidable that Apple is now a machine optimized for financial engineering, not necessarily future growth.

Final Takeaway

The company maintains a net cash position of $35 billion and holds $133 billion in cash and marketable securities, with $98 billion in debt. This fortress balance sheet gives it maneuvering room. But it's using that room less to build future revenue streams and more to shield its stock price. All of which makes the guidance for the June quarter feel precariously optimistic. Management expects revenue growth in the low to mid-single digits, assuming that macroeconomic conditions don’t deteriorate and that global tariff policies remain unchanged.

Those are bold assumptions.

U.S. GDP has softened. Bond yields are rising. Consumer leverage is increasing. And tariffs are not only back on the table, they are being used as strategic weapons in broader trade conflicts. Taken together, it’s a story of a company running at full tilt, executing flawlessly, and yet caught in the gravitational pull of an economic environment that is fundamentally misaligned with its business model.

From where I sit, Apple’s a Hold. The company just posted record earnings and solid cash flow, but the underlying macroeconomic forces are harder to ignore. Rising rates, tighter credit, and shaky consumer sentiment all press against a business that depends on people choosing to spend, not needing to. And with a valuation that looks stretched and a growing reliance on buybacks over breakthrough products, it’s tough to call this a buy until the macro clears up.

data.title: The Tariff "Pause" Won't Last: Here's Why
data.description:
NEW YORK (May 21) - The interim tariff deal between China and the United States in Geneva was released in the early morning hours, Eastern Time, of May 11th.

The deal lowered the US tariff on Chinese goods to 30% from 145% and rescinded China's retaliatory tariffs to 10% from 125%. Nevertheless, CNBC reports that retailers complain that "tariff stacking" on Chinese imports, including so-called "Section 301" tariffs for unfair trade practices, will push the tariff on a variety of consumer goods to near or above 50%.

According to 22 respondents to a Bloomberg survey of Asian, American, and European fund managers, the tariffs are likely to continue through 2025. Bloomberg Economics estimates that "the current rate is high enough to wipe out 70% of Chinese shipments" to the United States. Obviously, the tariff dispute will immediately affect retailers, as the Walmart (WMT) earnings call indicated. But as a bellwether for the retail industry overall, Wal*Mart CEO Doug McMillon's statement during the call is telling:

When it comes to the general merchandise categories that are impacted, we'll move production where that's possible. That isn't easy or fast, but we've been working on that for years, so it's not like we've just started to make adjustments.

It's not clear which categories of general merchandise McMillon thinks cannot possibly be relocated; they are likely very few. But the tariffs will also likely favorably affect transport, at least in the short term, as companies rush to ship orders before what is likely to be an indeterminate pause in the tariff dispute.

We've already seen that rush since the tariff dispute commenced in April. The Sea to Sky Cargo ETF (SEA), for example, is up 50% since the week tariffs were announced in April. While SEA holds a multinational portfolio of shipping companies, 14.5% of its shipping holdings (excluding cash and USG securities) are in Chinese and Hong Kong shipping companies. Importers from China use a variety of shipping companies to effect shipments in any event.

ANALYSIS

We don't believe the tariff issue will be resolved, although we think the pause will be extended, perhaps as far as year-end 2026. By then, we think many US importers will have moved Chinese production to other low-cost locations. But then all bets are off. As one US importer of Chinese kitchenware told the Financial Times, the 90-day reprieve...

...means the migration of production from China to our facilities in Cambodia and India could be just that - a migration, not an evacuation".

Toy maker VTech, with $893 million in North American sales in fiscal 2025, also announced it will move its production intended for the US market out of China. Others are likely to follow.

The Chinese also seem to think the armistice in the trade war won't last as well. As producers of goods intended for the US market migrate production from China, Chinese producers look for alternative markets to replace the US market, particularly Europe.

The Macroeconomics

The US chronic trade deficits, exacerbated in 2025 by the imports that were made in anticipation of tariffs, as illustrated in the chart below, clearly undermine the USA as a foreign investor, particularly the BRICS (Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa, as well as several others), move away from dollar assets.

Our goods trade deficit with China, nearly $300 billion in 2024, is a major part of that overall trade deficit. It must be resolved, one way or another, by more balanced trade or other means, including tariffs, that make up for the difference.

As the United States ramps up tariffs on China, the EU is expressing its own concerns that China's excess production capacity will be directed to Europe and exacerbate Europe's own trade deficit. As one analyst of Chinese political economy put it, the EU does not want to be the "shock absorber" for the China/US trade dispute:

"To mitigate the effect of US tariffs, China needs Europe to absorb more Chinese goods while Europe wants China to export less and import more," she added. "Their objectives are diametrically opposed."

The Much Larger Dispute

One would need to be deliberately obtuse to imagine that this tariff dispute is limited to economics and not by geopolitical and geostrategic interests. Those considerations are a major reason why we believe it will continue.

We know, already, for example, that twenty percentage points of the 30% current China tariff is to sanction China for its role in supplying the precursor chemicals used to create Fentanyl, a drug that killed nearly 50,000 Americans - mostly young people - in 2024. The 301 Tariffs are for chronic, proven, instances of unfair trade practices that were imposed because of chronic violations of WTO and other trade practices, including counterfeiting merchandise and industrial espionage.

According to the FBI,

The counterintelligence and economic espionage efforts emanating from the government of China and the Chinese Communist Party are a grave threat to the economic well-being and democratic values of the United States.

The United States and China are engaged in an adversarial - and, likely, inevitable - conflict that nearly always comes into play between rising powers and incumbent powers.

Political scientists call it the "Thucydides Trap", an eponym for the ancient historian's chronicle of the Peloponnesian Wars between Athens and Sparta in the Fifth Century, BC. It's a competitive scenario that has repeated several times among rising and existing powers throughout history. We're of the view that we're seeing it, yet again, as China's political leadership asserts what they believe to be China's "rightful place" in world affairs.

As part of this strategic competition, the leaders of China and the United States are making plays on the world stage to advance their respective economic interests - with accompanying geopolitical and geostrategic ramifications - over the other.

In recent weeks:

  • China's President Xi Jinping met with Russian President Vladimir Putin at Russia's Victory Day, commemorating Russia's role in the former Soviet Union that defeated the Axis Powers of World War II. In communiqués, the two discussed their mutual trade relationships and their messaging seemed to be it is their strategic intent to "stand together, even if Mr. Trump embraces Mr. Putin but pressures Mr. Xi", as the New York Times put it.
  • The United States considers Brazil to be strategically important to US interests as the other largest democracy in the Western Hemisphere. It has been a US military ally since 2019. The two countries share sensitive military intelligence and technology. Nevertheless, President Xi met with Brazil's President Lula earlier this month and, while much of the discussions reportedly involved trade and business issues, they also included Lula's meeting with China's state-owned defense behemoth China North Industries Corporation (Norinco) to discuss Norinco's possible investment in Avribas, a Brazilian aerospace and defense company that is struggling financially. The two also discussed Chinese investments in Brazilian ports and a strategically important commercial tunnel being built at the Port of Santos.
  • President Donald Trump's meetings in the Middle East last week seemed intended to bolster ties with important nations in the region, particularly Saudi Arabia, which counts China as its largest trading partner. The so-called "petrodollar", a keystone element of the US Dollar's standing as the world's reserve currency, is based largely on a 1974 accord with the kingdom to price its oil exports in USDs in exchange for US defense agreements. The BRICS nations, most prominently China, had attempted as recently as last year to establish a BRICS currency to compete with the USD and reduce its importance as a reserve. India, to its credit, expressed no interest and the notion was put aside. But China will continue to push to replace the USD with the yuan or some other proxy currency as part of its geopolitical and geostrategic effort to assert global dominance in its power struggle with the USA.

Summary: Why the Tariff Pause Won't Stand

Reporting this morning indicates that the wheels may come off this trade truce even before the 90-day pause is completed. And the rhetoric from both sides has continued unabated. While some of that is, undoubtedly, the kind of public posturing that occurs in any negotiation that draws public attention, it's clear there is no small element of animus between the two.

There is simply too deep a competition over strategic assets-be they metals, rare earths, ports, markets, or lesser nations' political leadership-between the United States and China to believe the tariff dispute or the balance of trade between the two countries will be resolved in a few months.

Investors should be listening carefully in earnings calls this quarter and next to assess whether management teams are taking steps to re-shore or nearshore their Chinese production to other locations if they source a significant portion of their products from China. Companies like Walmart (WMT), Apple (AAPL), and Nike, come to mind, as well as other companies and industries. (Bloomberg has used its ECAN tool to compile a chart showing the spike in reshoring and near-shoring discussions in earnings calls, indicating efforts are already underway to move production out of China). Companies like Cosmo Sourcing purport to help American companies source their goods from "Anywhere but China" to avoid the punitive Chinese tariffs.

Companies that derive a significant percentage of their revenues from Chinese markets, like PepsiCo, Starbucks, and McDonald's should anticipate those revenues will decline, slowly at first, but then more rapidly as China retaliates against the increased (and perhaps increasing) tariffs the Trump Administration will impose in its attempt to rebalance the US balance of payments with China.

But Don't Overlook Domestic Political Considerations Affecting the Tariff Dispute

Domestic political events in China or the United States could, of course, greatly alter our expectation that the tariff pause will end relatively soon.

First, there are signs of civil disquiet in China. The China Labor Bulletin collects reports of strikes, worker accidents, and worker calls for help and presents each type of incident on a map. It is based out of China, so subject to regime control. Nevertheless, it shows scores of the aforementioned types of incidents throughout China in each of the three types of incident maps.

Xi Jinping has also removed two of China's leading military figures from its seven-member Central Military Commission, China's "Joint Chiefs". There is thus a rare (and perhaps unprecedented) dual vacancy on the CMC. General Li Shangfu was dismissed in October 2023 and not yet replaced. General Admiral Miao Hua was removed in April. A third leader, and vice-chair of the CMC directly under Chair Xi Jinping, General He Weidong, has not been seen since March. China is, of course, opaque on the reasons for the removals and absences for national security purposes. While they most likely came about because of Xi Jinping's ongoing corruption probes, others have imputed the purges to unrest in China's senior military ranks that could, cause Xi Jinping to be toppled in a coup.

In the Western Hemisphere, the GOP holds only a narrow majority in the U.S. House of Representatives, and the Democratic caucus comprises many of the same members that impeached President Trump twice in his first term. If the tariffs trigger inflation, as many predict, or if geopolitical tensions with China escalate, House control may flip to the Democrats, who have been far less sympathetic to robust tariffs as trade policy.

For now, though, businesses and investors should brace for the steeper rates of tariff to be resumed no later than early to mid-2026 and possibly even earlier.

____________________________________

Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be under-performing and include strategies that we would recommend where the companies of our clients. Others discuss new management strategies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change).

data.title: PGIM Jennison Mid-Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

While the expectation that the broader investment landscape this year would be shaped by President Trump's agenda, greater uncertainty around implementation of his policies-trade and tariffs, tax reform, immigration, etc.-created new risks. While new tariffs began to be announced during the first quarter of 2025, it was unclear whether tariff threats were real or simply being used as a negotiating tactic. Market sentiment began to wane and volatility increased given heightened concerns about increased input costs leading to higher prices and a resurgence in inflation and the subsequent effect on consumer spending.

MONTH

The Russell Mid Cap Growth Index (the Index) returned - 7.4% in March with all sectors participating in the downside. Communication Services, Utilities, Information Technology (IT), and Consumer Discretionary lost the most ground.

The PGIM Jennison Mid-Cap Growth Fund (MUTF:PEGZX) underperformed the Index. Security selection within Information Technology detracted the most from relative performance. Stock selection within the Consumer Staples, Health Care, and Industrials sectors were additional areas of relative weakness. Stock selection within the Consumer Discretionary and Energy sectors, along with an overweight to Consumer Staples added the most value during the month.

QUARTER

The Fund lost value during the quarter and underperformed the −7.1% return of the Index. Stock selection within the Information Technology (driven by software and semiconductors) and Health Care (primarily life sciences tools & services) sectors detracted the most from relative performance. Security selection within Consumer Staples and Industrials were additional areas of relative weakness. On the upside, security selection within Communication Services (led by entertainment), Real Estate, and Consumer Discretionary (especially textiles, apparel & luxury goods) added the most value.

KEY CONTRIBUTORS

  • O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY)
  • Cheniere Energy (LNG)
  • GFL Environmental (GFL)

O'Reilly Automotive was buoyed by the potential for higher demand for aftermarket parts driven by expectations for lower new car sales due to tariff-induced price hikes as well as strong fourth quarter results. The team views O'Reilly as a best-in-class retailer in a fragmented industry offering solid growth prospects.

Cheniere Energy is an integrated, full-service liquefied natural gas (LNG) company that owns and operates LNG receiving terminals and pipelines in Louisiana and Texas. While fourth quarter results were mixed with strong free cash flow but somewhat lighter earnings, the outlook remains very positive. Its cost-effective capacity additions are a competitive advantage in our view.

GFL Environmental is a waste management company headquartered in Canada. The company reported solid results for its fourth quarter and raised revenue guidance for 2025. We believe the company can grow organically mid-single digits across all three business segments while margins expand driven by accretive merger & acquisition (M&A) and scale.

KEY DETRACTORS

  • Trade Desk (TTD)
  • Marvell Technology (MRVL)
  • Datadog (DDOG)

Trade Desk is an American multinational technology company that specializes in real-time programmatic marketing automation technologies, products, and services, designed to personalize digital content delivery to users. Shares declined sharply after the company reported its first earnings miss since going public in 2016. Operating expenses are also increasing which weighed on margins.

Marvell Technology's custom artificial intelligence chips and collaborations with major tech giants like Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG,GOOGL) are propelling advancements in its data center technology and AI infrastructure. Shares were down despite a modest beat in its first quarter results which fell short of heightened expectations that the initial volume from Amazon's Tranium2 chip would drive materially better Datacenter revenue.

Datadog operates in the fast-growing IT monitoring market, driven by increasing digital transformation and cloud adoption. Its platform creates a competitive edge, reducing the need for multiple tools and appealing to a wide range of customers. Shares declined due to slowing growth expectations and contracting margins.

PORTFOLIO OUTLOOK

As we look forward into 2025, challenges continue to intensify. The unpredictability of the new administration extends beyond economic policies, permeating a wide range of initiatives across its agenda. As such, the market volatility of the first quarter is unlikely to abate in the near term.

While the market was expecting an announcement on import tariffs, the magnitude of those tariffs is significantly higher than most had assumed. The subsequent sell-off in risk assets suggests the consensus belief that-if they hold-these tariff levels will be detrimental to U.S. economic growth and will likely put upward pressure on inflation. And while we have some clarity-at least for the time being-on percentages, new questions arise about their actual impact on consumer sentiment and spending, if there will be retaliation, and if announced rates hold or go up/down. None of these questions are answerable today and will likely continue to weigh on market sentiment and lead to concerns of an increased probability of recession.
