data.title: Our Green Zone Buy Signal Of April 7th Is Rapidly Winding Down
data.description:
On April 7th, we notified our subscribers that our Short-Term Master Sentiment Indicator (ST-MSI) was giving a Green Zone buy signal. We’ve indicated it using a green arrow. It was the first ST-MSI buy signal since November 2023.

The indicator is now rapidly approaching the Red Zone, which would be the first sell signal since January. If it does give a sell signal it will present us with a dilemma.

The Theory of Contrary Opinion and Why it Works

At the Sentiment King we believe in the theory of contrary opinion. The theory holds that stock prices go opposite what investors expect when “too many” investors have that expectation. If “too many” investors expect prices to rise, they'll decline. If “too many” expect prices to decline, they’ll rise.

The usual explanation of why the theory works, is this: if everyone is bullish, investors have already purchased, so even with more good news, there are few buyers left to push prices higher. The advance is “exhausted.” So just a little profit taking will move prices lower.

On the other hand, if everyone is bearish, they have already sold. So, even with more bad news, there are few sellers left to push prices lower. The market is “washed out.” So, just a little buying by bargain hunters will move prices higher.

It's a theory that's been used by every great investor throughout history. To apply it, however, you need some way to know when there are “too many.” That’s what the ST-MSI is for.

The ST-MSI

Over the years, numerous indicators have been developed to know when “too many” investors are expecting something. We combine seven of them into one indicator, which we call the ST-MSI or Short-Term Master Sentiment Indicator. It designed to signal intermediate-term moves that last from three to six months.

The names of the seven indicators are listed across the top of this table. The position of the composite ST-MSI is shown in blue in the far-right column.

The Red Zone, Green Zone Ranking System

The seven indicators are combined using our Red-Zone, Green-Zone ranking system. The Red Zone indicates when investors are “too bullish.” The Green Zone indicates when there are “too many” bears.

Putting all the indicators on the same scale allows us to combine them into one master indicator in a mathematically consistent way. The yellow X shows where the indicator currently ranks, while the plus sign is where it ranked a week earlier.

But our red zone green zone scale also helps us with something else.

Sentiment indicators only have meaning when they show extreme levels of investor expectations. Even though an indicator might show 70% of investors bullish, that might not be enough. The indicator might only have meaning when 90% are bullish. Our Red Green Zone scale tells us this.

Red and Green Zone readings tell us when historically that indicator is at an extreme. In between the red and Green Zone is a large gray area we call the neutral zone, where the indicator is not saying anything.

Contradictions

If the ST-MSI does give a Red Zone sell signal it will present us with a slight problem.

We wrote an article on May 13th (This advance should continue through the summer and move up in two waves) strongly suggesting this rally will continue through July and August. It was based on the long held Elliott wave principle that recovery rallies after a sharp selloff occur in two waves and that the market is still in the first wave. If we get an ST-MSI sell signal, it will contradict this. Which fact will prevail?

That remains to be seen. We update the ST-MSI once a week on Saturday for our subscribers. We don’t have a buy signal yet. These charts are for last week.

If we do get a sell signal, we’ll first discuss this contradiction with our subscribers and work out what, we think, will happen. At this time, we’re not sure.

data.title: XPeng Could Surge On Record Growth, AI Breakthroughs, And Wall Street's Blind Spot
data.description:
With how things are moving in the Electric Vehicle ("EV") market, I can’t help but spot a few investment-worthy candidates within the sector. Lucid Motors (LCID) and Rivian (RIVN) are some of the EV companies you can bet on for the long-haul, thanks mainly to the powerful entities backing them: Rivian has Amazon (AMZN), while Lucid has Saudi Arabia.

However, one of their peers, XPeng Inc. (XPEV) (OTCPK:XPNGF), stood out without such powerful backers - I mean, it technically has Volkswagen (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAGY) Groups backing it, but it isn’t the main selling point - at least not for me. Although XPeng is still not generating a profit, the stock is up 92% YTD.

Heads up: I’m rating XPEV stock a “Buy” - and not specifically because of its recent bull run, but because of how excited I am about this company's prospects once it turns a profit.

Company Overview

Guangzhou Xiaopeng Automotive Technology is a China-based car company that sets itself apart by producing “more advanced” electric vehicles, or Smart EVs. The company was founded in 2014 and went public in 2020 through an IPO on the New York Stock Exchange.

Initially, XPeng’s figurative “bread and butter” was the G3 and P7, which are crossover SUVs and 4-door sport sedans. However, XPeng stopped the production of its G3 platform in 2023.

Today, XPeng’s product lineup includes:

  • X9 (Flagship) - Electric MPVs

  • G9 - Luxury Electric SUVs

  • G6 - Mid-Size Electric SUVs

  • P7 - Electric Sedans.

These EVs are sold in 223 cities in roughly 700 physical stores worldwide. XPeng’s primary EVs are priced between ~$24,400 and ~$57,800. Today, the company’s primary market is Europe, Asia, and Latin America, while targeting the mid to high-end segment in China’s vehicle market.

If you’re wondering about the United States, no XPeng EV is sold here, at least not right now. So, if you did happen to spot one of these on the road, they're most likely imported from other countries.

Top-Line, Bottom-Line, and Earnings-Per-Share

On May 21, 2025, XPeng reported its Q1 ’25 financials, and the figures are compelling.

The company’s top line increased by over 140% year-over-year to around $2.2 billion. Although its operating earnings came out negative at $143.5 million, the loss improved by ~37% compared to the same period last year.

XPeng’s bottom line figures are similar: a net loss of $95 million for the quarter - ~51% lower than the previous year. Naturally, this improvement is reflected in XPeng's EPS for the quarter: ($0.05) from ($0.10). The annual figures paint a similar picture. XPeng’s top line for 2024 increased by nearly 30% year-over-year, operating loss declined by almost 40%, and net loss decreased by over 46% from 2023.

The gaps are far more notable when comparing FY’20 to FY’24 figures. Its top line increased by a whopping 525% to nearly $5.6 billion. However, its net loss nearly doubled over the same period.

Digging Deeper on XPeng’s Financials

While I have an idea, I can’t help but wonder: why is XPeng’s revenue growth so stellar, yet the rest of the crucial metrics aren’t as great?

It turns out XPeng's financial setbacks stem from higher costs of revenues, primarily driven by the extensive R&D and General Expenses, which is exactly my speculation on the company.

XPeng developed an in-house Operating System for its smart EVs and its own self-driving architecture, and they even collaborated with Volkswagen to develop superchargers. XPeng is definitely not sleeping on research and development. If XPeng were volume down on R&D expenses, we’d see better overall financials, but that can’t be the case right now. The company must sacrifice near-term profitability for long-term potential.

Just like its closest industry peers, Lucid and Rivian, XPeng is still in the early development stages. For investors, it means we really can’t expect much in terms of profitability - at least not right now.

For a better comparison, I’ve included 5-yr net income data from XPeng’s closest peers:

In my most recent analysis for Lucid, I pointed out the same occurrence of EV companies being unprofitable during their "early-stage struggles." And, as you can see from the peer group, pretty much of the group is operating in red, mainly due to R&D expenses.

So that explains the contrast between XPeng’s revenue growth vs other key metrics. But, looking closely, XPeng has the narrowest gap between net loss and profitability among its peers.

Sure, some in the peer group are now profitable, but if you look closely enough, it took them a long time to get there! Tesla, for example, turned a profit for the first time in 2020.

Then there’s Li Auto, who turned to profit following revenue increasing by 1,266% between 2020 and 2024 - the highest increase by % in the peer group.

XPeng Record Delivery Volumes

On the topic of XPeng’s revenue growth, it’s driven by an increase in vehicle sales. You might think I’m only stating the obvious, but follow me.

XPeng’s vehicle sales for Q1 ‘25 increased by nearly 160% year-over-year to nearly $2 billion. Total product deliveries increased by almost 331% to 94,008 units over the same period! By far, that’s the highest number of quarterly deliveries for XPeng. The momentum continued into April of this year with over 35,000 deliveries, which brought its YTD total deliveries close to 130,000 vehicles.

For perspective, Lucid Motors delivered 3,109 vehicles in Q1’25, up 58% year-over-year, while Rivian delivered 13,588 units with around 57% growth rate in the same quarter. I mean, I’m aware that the price range for XPeng EVs are notably lower than Lucid's or Rivian’s, so it shouldn’t be a surprise for the company to have more deliveries, right? …but I can’t help it, the gap is just insane.

With how product deliveries are going, I’m confident that XPeng will hit its Q2 ‘25 delivery target of 102,000 - 108,000 vehicles, which is a more or less 250% increase year-over-year. If that momentum continues, the company should be profitable in about a year.

XPEV Stock Performance

A few months after the IPO, XPEV hit its all-time high of $64.20 per share. From there, the stock saw wild swings on the backs of early investors trying to profit from the rally and then went on a notable decline in late 2022. Since then, volatility has come down.

Right now, the stock is down by over 65% from its all-time highs. Needless to say, I can feel your pain if you’re still holding a position that you bought 5 years back. That said, the same trend can be seen from XPeng’s closest peers as well. Based on the net income comparison I presented earlier, you’d understand why this huge decline isn’t entirely unique to XPeng.

Look at Rivian’s stock performance around the same period:

RIVN stock went from an all-time high of around $130 to $16.34 - that’s a near 88% decline!

On the other hand, Lucid Motors isn't very different. LCID declined from all-time highs of around $56 to $2.77, which means a nearly 95% decline.

Even more established companies within its peer groups experienced the same downwards trend, with Tesla being an exception - that’s primarily because of how Tesla investors perceive CEO Elon Musk.

Going back to XPeng. Although XPEV price movements are primarily driven by macroeconomic forces - its 52-week price movement offers a more compelling view compared to its peers:

More importantly, XPEV stock is up by 92% Year-to-Date, while Lucid Motors and Tesla are down by around 9% and 17%, respectively. Considering that most tech and EV companies retracted in the same period, this is just impressive.

Wall Street analysts also echoed bullish sentiments for XPeng. Although I don’t really dive into these experts’ ratings, XPEV just happens to be one of the Wall Street darlings right now - consensus among 29 analysts rate the stock as a “Buy.”

Potential Catalysts for XPeng

There’s lots of positive things to talk about when it comes to XPeng, and some of these are the reasons behind my ‘buy rating’ - despite the bottlenecks.

The company’s most immediate catalyst is, arguably, the newly launched 2025 XPeng X9 - a flagship model with Turing AI Smart Driving as a standard feature. That’s a leap for the company’s autonomous driving capabilities. XPeng management highlighted the ‘accelerated adoption of physical AI technologies’ as a potential growth driver. Although it remains to be seen, the company’s emphasis on AI integration with the XPeng vehicle lineup means there’s a substantial investment to improve autonomous driving functions.

Also, CEO Xiaopeng He emphasized launching "more blockbuster products," which potentially means there will be more product introductions in the following years. I mean, we know how good XPeng is when it comes to selling their products - it even outpaced NIO in terms of vehicle deliveries.

XPeng’s partnership with Volkswagen also presents potential opportunities. In fact, the reported revenues outside vehicle sales - or ‘other services’ are said to be attributable to the platform and technical collaboration between XPeng and Volkswagen Group. Revenue from these segments contributes to the overall profitability while the company scales up vehicle production. Although I don’t know how long the partnership will last, XPeng got its hands on Volkswagen’s technologies, which are highly competitive in the EV business.

Tesla’s eroding market share also presents an opportunity for companies like XPeng, though certainly in the United States anytime soon. I’ve always been vocal about my take on Tesla, and how Elon Musk’s direct involvement with politics is affecting the company.

Personally, I know Tesla owners who sold their EVs because they don’t want to be associated with what’s happening with the controversies surrounding the CEO. It’s a more critical decision considering that protesters from different parts of the world are targeting Tesla properties and showrooms.

In short, Tesla’s reputation is definitely not in good shape right now, which is why deliveries and production are declining.

More importantly, despite Tesla’s setbacks, there’s been a surge in EV sales, especially in China - where XPeng and most of its closest peers originate. This is why I think Tesla’s not-so-good situation benefits other EVs, which are often more affordable, have a better price-to-value ratio, aesthetics, range, and, most importantly, no resentment from Tesla protesters. You can sleep well at night knowing your car won’t be targeted by some angry mob at the stop light.

My Verdict

Companies like XPeng aren't exactly the type of company that I'm known to bet the farm on. As an income investor, I’d naturally be comfortable investing in companies with time-tested business models and stable dividend income.

That said, diversification is something that investors must do, and XPEV is definitely a stock worth-considering. The company is ramping up production as a result of incredibly high demand. And although it’s still operating at a loss, it’s closest to profitability compared to its peers.

If XPeng’s momentum continues and targets are realized - which is quite likely to happen with how things are moving - I think investors will be very sorry if they missed out on buying a position at these prices.
